target.name: Pyrrolizidine Alkaloids
target.motivation: The synthesis of pyrrolizidine alkaloids (-)-Trachelantamidine (21) and (-)-Pseudoheliotridane (23) demonstrates a significant application of monofluoromethyl radical-mediated XAT, offering a less toxic alternative to traditional methods involving tin radicals or depleting transition metals.

prior_syntheses.key_strategy: Copper(I) catalyzed radical atom transfer cyclization

success_criteria.free_text: Achieve total synthesis of (-)-Trachelantamidine (21) and (-)-Pseudoheliotridane (23) using the novel XAT method, demonstrating good overall yields and avoiding toxic reagents.

key_disconnections.reasoning: The key step involves the cyclization of amide 18 to form the pyrrolizidine core 19 via an intramolecular atom transfer radical addition, mediated by the monofluoromethyl radical. This avoids the use of toxic tin radicals or transition metals previously employed for this transformation.
The acetate group in 20 needs to be removed to obtain the alcohol 21. This is a standard deprotection step.
To obtain the dehalogenated intermediate 22, the iodine atom in 19 needs to be removed. This is achieved via hydrogen atom transfer.
The amide carbonyl in 22 needs to be reduced to an amine to form the final pyrrolizidine alkaloid 23. This is a common reduction strategy for amides.

retrosynthetic_strategy.rationale: The overall strategy focuses on leveraging the monofluoromethyl radical-mediated XAT for the crucial cyclization step, followed by standard functional group transformations to reach the target natural products. This approach aims to provide a more sustainable and less toxic route compared to existing methods.

thought.background: Halogen-atom transfer (XAT) is an effective method for generating carbon radicals from alkyl halides. Monofluoromethyl radicals have been identified as potent nucleophilic XAT reagents, capable of initiating atom transfer radical addition (ATRA) reactions with unactivated alkenes. This method avoids the need for photocatalysts and toxic reagents.
thought.gap: Can this novel monofluoromethyl radical-mediated XAT/ATRA methodology be applied to the synthesis of complex natural products, specifically pyrrolizidine alkaloids, which traditionally rely on less desirable radical sources?
thought.decision: To demonstrate the versatility and utility of the monofluoromethyl radical-mediated XAT, we will attempt the total synthesis of (-)-Trachelantamidine (21) and (-)-Pseudoheliotridane (23), focusing on the intramolecular cyclization of an N-allylamide as a key step.

evidence.text_span: A significant application of monofluoromethyl radical-mediated XAT was demonstrated in the total synthesis of natural products 21 and 23, as shown in Scheme 7B. The crucial step in this synthesis was the cyclization of amide 18, which proceeded successfully in good yield on a gram scale. Previously, this transformation had only been achieved using toxic tin radicals or depleting transition metals.18,70−74 Our method allowed for the successful completion of the syntheses of (−)-Trachelantamidine (21) and (−)-Pseudoheliotridane (23), resulting in overall yields of 22 and 26%, respectively.

thought.background: The monofluoromethyl radical-mediated XAT/ATRA has been optimized for various alkyl halides and alkenes. The key cyclization step for pyrrolizidine alkaloids involves an intramolecular ATRA of an N-allylamide.
thought.gap: Will the intramolecular cyclization of (S)-18 proceed efficiently under the established conditions, and will the desired pyrrolizidine core 19 be formed?
thought.decision: To form the pyrrolizidine core, (S)-18 will be subjected to the optimized reaction conditions: PhI(OCOCH2F)2 (3, 0.5 equiv.) in DCM (0.1 M) under an argon atmosphere, irradiated with violet LEDs (400 nm) at room temperature for 16 hours, expecting the formation of 19.

outcome.observation: The cyclization of amide 18 proceeded successfully, yielding the pyrrolizidine core 19 in good yield on a gram scale.

evidence.text_span: The crucial step in this synthesis was the cyclization of amide 18, which proceeded successfully in good yield on a gram scale.

thought.background: The pyrrolizidine core 19, containing an iodine atom, has been successfully synthesized. To progress towards (-)-Trachelantamidine (21), the iodine needs to be replaced with an oxygen-containing functional group, specifically an acetate, which can later be hydrolyzed to a hydroxyl group.
thought.gap: Can the iodine in 19 be selectively substituted with an acetate group to form 20?
thought.decision: To introduce the acetate group, 19 will be reacted with silver acetate in acetic acid at elevated temperature, expecting the formation of 20.

outcome.observation: Product 20 was obtained in good yield.

thought.background: The acetate intermediate 20 has been successfully synthesized. The final step to obtain (-)-Trachelantamidine (21) is the reduction of the acetate to a primary alcohol.
thought.gap: Can the acetate group in 20 be selectively reduced to a hydroxyl group without affecting the amide functionality?
thought.decision: To convert the acetate to a hydroxyl group, 20 will be treated with lithium aluminum hydride in THF under reflux conditions, expecting the formation of 21.

thought.background: The pyrrolizidine core 19, containing an iodine atom, has been successfully synthesized. To progress towards (-)-Pseudoheliotridane (23), the iodine needs to be removed, and the amide carbonyl needs to be reduced.
thought.gap: Can the iodine in 19 be selectively removed via hydrogen atom transfer to form 22?
thought.decision: To remove the iodine, 19 will be subjected to hydrogen atom transfer using tris(trimethylsilyl)silane (TTMSS) under photochemical conditions, expecting the formation of 22.

outcome.observation: The dehalogenated product 22 was obtained in high yield.

evidence.text_span: TTMSS (1.3 equiv.), MeCN, RT, 6h, Violet LEDs (400 nm), 82%

thought.background: The dehalogenated amide 22 has been successfully synthesized. The final step to obtain (-)-Pseudoheliotridane (23) is the reduction of the amide carbonyl to an amine.
thought.gap: Can the amide carbonyl in 22 be selectively reduced to an amine to form 23?
thought.decision: To reduce the amide, 22 will be treated with borane-dimethyl sulfide complex in THF under an argon atmosphere, expecting the formation of 23.

validation.comparison_to_prior: The developed method provides a less toxic and simpler alternative for the synthesis of pyrrolizidine alkaloids compared to previous methods that relied on tin radicals or transition metals.
validation.significance: This work demonstrates the first successful application of a simple carbon-centered radical as an efficient XAT reagent under photochemical conditions for the total synthesis of complex natural products. It offers a more sustainable and atom-economical approach to these valuable compounds.

source.title: Monofluoromethyl Radical Mediated Halogen-Atom Transfer.
source.authors: Ramkumar N, Raiskuma AP, Baumane L, Belyakov S, Traskovskis K, Veliks J.
